matchup: San Antonio Spurs at Oklahoma City Thunder
core_read: San Antonio won Game 1 by controlling the glass, sustaining rim/3 shot quality, and surviving heavy turnover pressure. OKC generated enough scoring volume and three-point efficiency to stay live, but the rebounding deficit and foul disadvantage created a structural Game 1 loss profile.

Victor Wembanyama.diagnostic: Series-defining glass control. OKC has no clean one-on-one rebounding answer if Wembanyama stays near 49 minutes.

Dylan Harper.diagnostic: Guard rebound tail was real, not noise. He captured long misses and helped SAS survive OKC perimeter volume.

Julian Champagnie.diagnostic: Weak-side forward rebound stabilizer. Important for Game 2 corner/above-break miss recovery.

Chet Holmgren.diagnostic: High opportunity, poor conversion. OKC's biggest Game 2 correction lever is Chet converting more of his chances.

Cason Wallace.diagnostic: Backcourt rebounding overperformance but low absolute chance base.

Shai Gilgeous-Alexander.diagnostic: Low rebound conversion despite massive minutes. Avoid SGA rebound overs unless Game 2 role shifts away from pure creation burden.

SAS_primary_unit.diagnostic: This was the series-winning identity group: elite shot profile and massive glass control, but turnover risk remains the primary liability.

OKC_primary_unit.diagnostic: Best clean-control OKC group. Strong scoring efficiency and zero turnovers, but still did not solve the SAS rebounding edge.

OKC_Caruso_SGA_Wiggins_JWill_Wallace.diagnostic: Spacing/creation failure group. Should be downgraded for Game 2 unless used only as defensive bridge.

shot_profile_diagnostics.SAS: San Antonio generated an elite rim-or-three profile at 84.38%, including 38 rim attempts and 43 threes. They were not merely hot; the shot diet was structurally strong.
shot_profile_diagnostics.OKC: OKC shot better from three at 37.78% and made 17 threes, but only 72.28% rim-or-three frequency and weaker rim volume reduced pressure consistency.
shot_profile_diagnostics.key_delta: SAS converted game control through possession volume and free throws, not pure eFG dominance. OKC's eFG was close, but SAS owned the possession economy.

four_factor_proxy.turnover_edge: OKC advantage: SAS 23 TOs vs OKC 15 TOs, but OKC failed to fully monetize it because SAS dominated rebounds and foul pressure.
four_factor_proxy.rebound_edge: Massive SAS advantage: +20 total rebounds, +7 offensive rebounds, +15.51 percentage-point FG DREB edge.
four_factor_proxy.free_throw_edge: SAS advantage: OKC committed 26 fouls and SAS drew 26 fouls; SAS generated 27 FT points in scoring table vs OKC 16.

blind_spots: SAS turnover count did not kill them because rebound dominance offset it
OKC Chet rebound chances were high but conversion was poor
SGA rebound conversion suppressed despite 51+ minutes
SAS guard/wing rebound distribution was stronger than expected

patch_queue.trigger: If Wembanyama projects 44+ minutes with 30+ rebound chances, raise SAS rebound margin and downgrade opposing center rebound conversion unless market already prices 18+ boards.
If Chet records 18+ rebound chances but sub-45% adjusted chance conversion, Game 2 rebound projection gets mild upward correction but capped against Wembanyama lineups.
Do not auto-fade SAS for high turnovers when they hold +12 or better rebound margin and draw +5 or more foul differential.
If OKC commits 24+ fouls or gives SAS 24+ drawn fouls, downgrade OKC spread and late-game closeout probability.
When Vassell-Champagnie-Wembanyama-Castle-Harper lineup exceeds 25 minutes and rim-or-three frequency is 85%+, treat SAS offensive profile as sustainable, not shooting luck.

prop_watchlist.lean: Over only if line <= 7.5 and price fair; otherwise no-call due Wemby suppression
Over only if line <= 116.5 and foul discipline correction assumed

game2_prep.game2_strategy: Do not chase revenge narrative blindly. OKC has correction paths through Chet rebound conversion and cleaner SGA-led lineups, but SAS possession economy is real. Prioritize rebound markets and full-game total bands before spread unless market overreacts to SAS Game 1 win.
